subject: Government
q: The civil service cadre responsible for the general supervision and control of ministries is the
explanation: In a civil service system, the administrative cadre is responsible for the overall supervision and control of ministries. These civil servants are senior officers who manage the day-to-day operations of government departments. They ensure that policies are implemented and administrative functions run smoothly. They often provide leadership and guidance to other staff in the civil service.

subject: Government
q: A disadvantage of the one-party system is that it
explanation: A one-party system can restrict or completely eliminate the ability of individuals to form or join different political parties, which diminishes the freedom of association. In such a system, opposition parties are either banned or heavily suppressed, leading to a lack of political pluralism and the stifling of political debate.

subject: Government
q: Citizenship refers to the
options: social status of a person in a state
legal status of a member of a state
explanation: Citizenship is the legal relationship between an individual and a state, granting the person certain rights, responsibilities and privileges. It implies that a person is a recognised member of the state and subject to its laws, and in turn, is entitled to the protection and benefits provided by the state.

subject: Government
q: The rule of law is a cardinal principle associated with
explanation: The rule of law is a fundamental principle associated with democratic systems. In a democratic system, the rule of law means that everyone, including government officials, is subject to and accountable under the law. This means that laws are applied consistently and fairly and ensures that individuals and institutions are governed by a set of rules rather than the arbitrary decisions of those in power. Democratic systems respect the rule of law to safeguard individual rights, promote justice and prevent the abuse of power.

subject: Government
q: The main function of public opinion is to
explanation: Public opinion shapes the direction of government policies. It reflects the views, attitudes and preferences of the general population on various issues, and governments often consider public opinion when formulating policies to ensure they align with the values and interests of the people.

subject: Government
q: A typical form of delegated legislation is
explanation: A bye-law is a form of delegated (or secondary) legislation, where the power to make laws is delegated by the legislature to a local authority or an organisation to make regulations within a certain area or for specific purposes. These laws apply to local or specific issues and are subordinate to primary legislation (acts of parliament).

subject: Government
q: A referendum is a device to ensure that
options: legislators vote to resolve contentious issues
bye - elections are held to fill vacant positions
decisions are effected with the consent of citzens
explanation: A referendum is a process in which the government seeks the direct approval or disapproval of the electorate on a specific issue. Such issues may include constitutional amendments, major policy changes or other significant decisions. It ensures that decisions are made with the consent of citizens.

subject: Government
q: One main weakness of a unitary system of government is that
options: it facilitates the domination of minority groups
the constitution can be amended easily
it is run as a single entity
carpet - crossing and opposition are made difficult
explanation: In a unitary system of government, power is concentrated at the national level, and local governments, if they exist, have limited powers that can be revoked by the central government. This centralisation of authority can lead to the domination of minority groups, as the central government may not fully represent the interests of all regions or minority populations, particularly those in less politically dominant areas.

topics.title: The Workings of Government II - Unitary, Federal & Confederal Structures

subject: Government
q: Pressure groups harmonise different individual concerns through interest
explanation: Pressure groups aggregate individual concerns by bringing together various individual interests and combining them into a collective stance or platform. This allows them to present a unified position on a particular issue to influence public policy or government decisions.

subject: Government
q: The act of removing an elected official by the electorate is referred to as
explanation: Recall is the act of removing an elected official from office before their term has ended, usually through a direct vote by the electorate. It allows citizens to hold their elected representatives accountable for their actions while in office. An impeachment, on the other hand, is the process by which the legislature removes an elected official from office if found guilty of gross misconduct.

subject: Government
q: One of the major sources of a constitution is
explanation: Judicial precedence (also known as case law or stare decisis) is a major source of a constitution. Courts follow previous judicial decisions when making rulings on similar cases, and over time, these decisions can become part of the constitutional framework.

subject: Government
q: A characteristic of public opinion is that it is
explanation: Public opinion is considered dynamic because it changes over time in response to various factors such as political events, social movements, economic conditions and media influence. It is not fixed or static; rather, it evolves and fluctuates as new information or issues emerge.

subject: Government
q: Coalition government arises when
options: one of the parties has a majority in parliament
no party has a majority in parliament
two or more parties co - operate to pass a bill in parliament
the ruling party is defeated in parliament
explanation: A coalition government arises when no single political party has enough seats to form a majority in parliament. In such cases, two or more parties come together to form a government by sharing power and collaborating to achieve a majority in the legislature.

subject: Government
q: One of the central tenets of the fascist doctrine is that the leader is
options: subordinate to the norms of the society
subordinate to the laws of the state
explanation: In fascism, the leader (often referred to as the Führer or Duce) is considered supreme and above the constitution, laws and norms of society. Fascist ideology emphasises the absolute authority of the leader, who is seen as the embodiment of the state and its will. This authoritarian system often disregards democratic processes, the rule of law and constitutional limits on power.

subject: Government
q: Which of the following electoral bodies in Nigeria conducted elections from 1979 to 1983?
explanation: The Federal Electoral Commission (FEDECO) was the electoral body responsible for conducting elections in Nigeria from 1979 to 1983. FEDECO was established to oversee the electoral processes during the Second Republic, and it conducted the general elections that brought in civilian government in 1979, as well as the subsequent elections up until the military takeover in 1983.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Institutions of Government in Post-Independence Nigeria

subject: Government
q: Constitutionalism refers to the
explanation: Constitutionalism is the principle that government should operate within the limits and framework set by a constitution. It is adherence to the constitution as a guiding authority. It means that the actions of the government remain within the boundaries of constitutional law.

subject: Government
q: Under the Presidential system of government the legislature and the executive are
options: elected separately to a fixed term
elected separately to an unfixed term
appointed by the judiciary to a fixed term
appointed at the same time to an unfixed term
explanation: In a Presidential system of government, the legislature and the executive (the President) are elected independently of each other. Both branches serve fixed terms: for example, in Nigeria, the President and members of the House of Assembly serve fixed terms of four years. This separation ensures that the executive and legislative branches operate independently, preventing any undue influence from one branch over the other and supporting the principle of checks and balances.

topics.title: The Workings of Government I - Organs & Systems

subject: Government
q: The Supreme Court, through its interpretations of the constitution, is a
options: participator in the politics of the state
explanation: The Supreme Court, by interpreting the Constitution, acts as a guardian of freedom by protecting the rights and liberties of individuals. Through its rulings, the Court upholds constitutional principles and ensures that laws and governmental actions do not infringe on the fundamental rights and freedoms guaranteed to citizens.

topics.title: The Workings of Government I - Organs & Systems

subject: Government
q: Separation of powers is a principle which enables each arm of government to
options: overlap the functions of the other
moderate the scope of the constitution
explanation: Separation of powers is a principle that allows each arm of government—the executive, the legislature, and the judiciary—to function independently and carry out its designated roles as outlined by the Constitution. This principle helps to prevent any one branch from exercising too much power by ensuring that each branch operates within its own defined responsibilities.

subject: Government
q: The main attributes of a state are
options: the press, the legislature, the executive and the judiciary
government, the police and the armed forces
explanation:
These are considered the four essential attributes of a state:

  1. Population – The people who make up the state.
  2. Territory – The defined geographical area within which the state has authority.
  3. Government – The organised institution or system through which state policies are created and enforced.
  4. Sovereignty – The supreme authority of the state to govern itself and make independent decisions without external interference.

subject: Government
q: The General Purpose Committee of the local government functions as a
options: body responsible for supervising self-help projects
committee of the government on public relations
explanation: The General Purpose Committee in local government functions similarly to a cabinet for the local government. It is responsible for making major policy decisions and overseeing the administration's key activities. This committee typically includes the heads of departments and senior officials, who help coordinate various functions of the local government, much like a cabinet in larger governmental structures.

subject: Government
q: Nigeria observed the principle of collective responsibility between
explanation: Between 1960 and 1966, Nigeria operated a parliamentary system of government. In this system, the Prime minister and his cabinet are collectively responsible for the policies that are implemented. This system was later replaced by the presidential system of government in the Second Republic.

topics.title: The Workings of Government I - Organs & Systems
Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Constitutional Development in Nigeria II - Independence & Post-Independence

subject: Government
q: The political party with the widest spread membership in Nigeria during the Second Republic was the
explanation: During Nigeria's Second Republic (1979–1983), the National Party of Nigeria (NPN) was the political party with the widest spread of membership across the country. The NPN aimed to establish a national presence by appealing to diverse ethnic and regional groups, which contributed to its popularity and success in multiple regions. It was the ruling party, and it won the presidential elections in both 1979 and 1983 with Shehu Shagari as its candidate.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: Military intervention in Nigeria arose from
options: perceived incapacity of civilians to govern
civilian desire to give up power
explanation: In many cases of military intervention including in Nigeria, the military justifies its actions by claiming that the civilian government is unable to effectively govern or maintain order. This perception of civilian incapacity to govern often serves as a rationale for military intervention, as the military may see itself as a more capable or necessary force to address perceived problems or crises in the country.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: Federalism was introduced in Nigeria under the
explanation: Federalism was introduced in Nigeria under the Lyttleton Constitution of 1954. This constitution formally established Nigeria as a federal state by dividing the country into regions with distinct administrative powers. This marled a shift from a unitary system to a federal one. It also created a federal parliament with a central government and regional governments, each having its own legislature.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Constitutional Development in Nigeria I - Pre-Independence

subject: Government
q: Regional consciousness in Nigeria was introduced by the
explanation: Regional consciousness in Nigeria was formally introduced by the Richards Constitution of 1946. This constitution created three regions—the Northern, Western, and Eastern regions—which were given a degree of administrative autonomy.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Constitutional Development in Nigeria I - Pre-Independence

subject: Government
q: How many states were created in Nigeria in 1967?
explanation:
On the 5th of May,1967, Nigeria's military government, led by General Yakubu Gowon, reorganised the country from its four regions (Northern, Eastern, Western and Mid-Western Regions) into 12 states. This restructuring aimed to address ethnic and political tensions, particularly in response to the growing secessionist movement in the Eastern Region, which later led to the Nigerian Civil War. The 12 states created in 1967 were:

  1. North-Western State
  2. North-Eastern State
  3. Kano State
  4. Kaduna State
  5. Kwara State
  6. Benue-Plateau State
  7. Lagos State
  8. Western State
  9. Mid-Western State
  10. East-Central State
  11. Rivers State
  12. South-Eastern State

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The day-to-day operation of public corporations is the direct responsibility of the
explanation: The management of a public corporation is directly responsible for its day-to-day operations. This includes overseeing the daily activities and making decisions about the corporation.

subject: Government
q: Under the independence Constitution of Nigeria, Dr Nnamdi Azikiwe was
explanation:
Under Nigeria's Independence Constitution of 1960, Dr. Nnamdi Azikiwe became the Governor-General of Nigeria. He was the ceremonial head of state, representing the British monarch (Queen Elizabeth II), as Nigeria was still a constitutional monarchy at that time.

In 1963, after Nigeria became a republic, Dr. Azikiwe became the first President of Nigeria under the new republican constitution, which removed the monarchy and established the office of the president as the head of state.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Constitutional Development in Nigeria II - Independence & Post-Independence

subject: Government
q: Which of the following contributed greatly to the introduction of the Clifford Constitution?
options: National Congress of British West Africa
explanation: The National Congress of British West Africa (NCBWA) was a congress formed in Accra, Ghana and consisted of Nigerian and Ghanaian delegates who were sent to London to represent Nigeria's interest in the issue of granting elective principle. Their resolution led to the granting of elective representation for Africans as stipulated in the Clifford constitution of 1922.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Constitutional Development in Nigeria I - Pre-Independence

subject: Government
q: In Nigeria, the Local Government Service Commission is set up by the
explanation: In Nigeria, the Local Government Service Commission (LGSC) is established by the state government. The commission is responsible for the recruitment, promotion and discipline of local government staff in the respective states. It ensures that local government employees are properly managed and maintained and that they are operating within the frameworks set by state governments in line with the Constitution and local government laws.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: In the 1930s, the political movement that challenged the NNDP domination of Lagos politics was the
explanation: In the 1930s, the National Youth Movement (NYM) was the political movement that challenged the NNDP (Nigerian National Democratic Party) domination of Lagos politics. The NNDP, led by Herbert Macaulay, was the dominant political force in Lagos at the time, but the NYM, led by Nnamdi Azikiwe, aimed to promote more nationalist and progressive political ideals. The NYM advocated for greater self-government for Nigerians and was a precursor to the formation of more significant political parties like the NCNC.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The Nigerian Public Complaints Commission as the Ombudsman suffers most from
options: poor knowledge of the workings of the public service
too many complaints to handle effectively
lack of power to enforce its decisions
explanation: The Nigerian Public Complaints Commission (PCC), which functions as the Ombudsman for the country, faces a significant challenge in its lack of enforcement power. While the commission is tasked with investigating complaints from citizens regarding the maladministration or misconduct of public officials, it lacks the legal authority to compel government agencies or officials to implement its recommendations or decisions. This limitation severely hampers its ability to effectively address and resolve complaints.

subject: Government
q: Residual powers in the 1979 Constitution of Nigeria were vested in the
explanation: In the 1979 constitution, the legislative powers were divided among the federal, state and local governments. The federal government, through the parliament, could legislate on items on the exclusive legislative list, while both the federal and state governments could legislate on items under the concurrent legislative list. The residual powers resided in the hands of the local government.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Constitutional Development in Nigeria II - Independence & Post-Independence

subject: Government
q: A characteristic of most pre-colonial governments in Nigeria is that they
options: had no clear separation of powers
explanation: Most pre-colonial governments in Nigeria, such as those of the Yoruba, Igbo, Hausa-Fulani, and other ethnic groups, did not have a formal separation of powers like modern democratic systems. The rulers or leaders often held a combination of executive, legislative and judicial functions. For instance, in some societies, the king or chief not only enacted laws but also enforced them and settled disputes.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: Prior to the formation of the OAU in 1963, Nigeria identified with the
explanation: Prior to the formation of the Organization of African Unity (OAU) in 1963, Nigeria was aligned with the Monrovia Group. This group was composed of African nations that had a more moderate approach to African unity. They were favouring cooperation without a centralised union.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations I - OAU, AU, ECOWAS, APPO

subject: Government
q: In 1991, the African Economic Community Treaty was signed in
explanation: The African Economic Community (AEC) Treaty was signed in Abuja, Nigeria, in 1991. The treaty aimed to establish a pan-African economic community through the gradual integration of existing regional economic communities, ultimately leading to the creation of a single economic and political union across the continent.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community

subject: Government
q: The first African Secretary General of the United Nations is
explanation: The first African Secretary-General of the United Nations was an Egyptian politician and diplomat, Boutrous Boutrous Ghali, who served as the sixth Secretary-General of the United Nations (UN) from 1992 to 1996.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations II- League of Nations, UNO/ UN, OPEC, The Commonwealth of Nations

subject: Government
q: Nigerians opposed the Defence Pact with Britain at independence because it
options: was forced on Nigeria, by Britian
was of no benefit to Nigeria
explanation: At the time of Nigeria's independence in 1960, the Defence Pact with Britain was viewed by many Nigerians as an affront to the country's sovereignty. The agreement allowed Britain to have a military presence in Nigeria and intervene in its affairs if necessary. For many Nigerians, this was seen as a symbol of continued colonial influence. The pact was perceived as undermining Nigeria's pride as a newly independent nation.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community

subject: Government
q: The Technical Aid Corps scheme in Nigeria aims at strengthening relations with
explanation: The Technical Aid Corps (TAC) scheme in Nigeria aims at strengthening relations with African countries. It was a scheme to develop and strengthen relations with the African, the Caribbean, and the Pacific States (ACP countries). The program sends Nigerian experts in various fields, including education, medicine, engineering and agriculture, to assist in the development of these countries.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community

subject: Government
q: Nigeria's quest for a leadership role in Africa hinges principally on her
explanation: Nigeria's economic strength plays an important role in its pursuit of leadership in Africa. A strong economy enables Nigeria to invest in development projects, provide aid to other African nations, and engage in regional initiatives, thereby bolstering its influence and leadership position on the continent.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community

subject: Government
q: Which of the following world powers was the first to come to Nigeria's aid during the civil war?
explanation: During the Nigerian Civil War (1967–1970), Britain was the first world power to come to Nigeria's aid. They supported the Federal Government's military efforts. This support was largely driven by political, economic, and strategic interests, including the desire to maintain relations with Nigeria as a former colony and the geopolitical situation during the Cold War.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community

subject: Government
q: Nigeria's relations with African countries are underscored by its policy of
explanation: Nigeria's relationship with African countries is often characterised by Afrocentrism, which emphasises the importance of Africa's unity, cultural heritage and the need for African solutions to African problems. Nigeria has consistently advocated for the promotion of African interests, peace and economic cooperation within the continent, and it has played an active role in organisations like the African Union (AU) and the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS).

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community

subject: Government
q: The foreign policy thrust of the Babangida administration was
explanation:
The foreign policy thrust of the Babangida administration (1985–1993) in Nigeria was economic diplomacy. This approach focused on using Nigeria’s foreign policy to promote economic interests, attract foreign investment, and secure international support for economic reforms, especially during the implementation of the Structural Adjustment Programme (SAP).

Economic diplomacy involved:

  • Engaging international financial institutions like the IMF and World Bank

  • Encouraging foreign partnerships

  • Promoting exports and economic cooperation

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community

subject: Government
q: One of the principal objectives of the OPEC is to
options: harmonize the oil policies of member countries
determine oil prices in the international market
explanation: One of the principal objectives of the Organisation of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) is to harmoni and coordinate the oil policies of its member countries. This helps to ensure stable oil prices and to manage the production and supply of oil in a way that benefits the economies of the member states.

topics.title: International Organisations II- League of Nations, UNO/ UN, OPEC, The Commonwealth of Nations

subject: Government
q: How many countries constitute the Economic Community of West African States?
explanation:
As of now, the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) consists of 15 member countries. These are:

  1. Benin
  2. Burkina Faso
  3. Cape Verde
  4. Ivory Coast
  5. Ghana
  6. Guinea
  7. Guinea-Bissau
  8. Liberia
  9. Mali
  10. Niger
  11. Nigeria
  12. Senegal
  13. Sierra Leone
  14. Togo
  15. Gambia

topics.title: International Organisations I - OAU, AU, ECOWAS, APPO

subject: Government
q: The charter of the United Nations was drawn up in
explanation: The Charter of the United Nations was drawn up in San Francisco in 1945 during the United Nations Conference on International Organisation. Representatives from 50 countries gathered to create the UN's foundational document, which laid out the principles and structure of the organisation.

topics.title: International Organisations II- League of Nations, UNO/ UN, OPEC, The Commonwealth of Nations

subject: Government
q: The two leaders that played the most prominent roles in the formation of the ECOWAS were
explanation: Nigeria, under the leadership of General Yakubu Gowon and Togo, under President Eyadema, initiated the formation of ECOWAS. It was founded on 28th May, 1975 in Lagos, Nigeria.

topics.title: International Organisations I - OAU, AU, ECOWAS, APPO

subject: Government
q: The Secretary General of the OAU is appointed by the
options: Council upon the recommendation of the Assembly
Council upon the recommendation of the Secretariat
Assembly upon the recommendation of the council
Defence Commission upon the recommendation of the secretariat
explanation: The Secretary General of the Organization of African Unity (OAU), which has since been replaced by the African Union (AU), was appointed by the Assembly upon the recommendation of the Council. The Assembly is the highest decision-making body of the OAU, and the Council, which consists of foreign ministers or representatives of the member states, would recommend a candidate for the position.

topics.title: International Organisations I - OAU, AU, ECOWAS, APPO

subject: Government
q: The approval of budgetary and financial matters in the United Nations is the responsibility of the
explanation:
The General Assembly approves budgetary and financial matters. It approves the UN's regular budget and determines the financial contributions of each member state.

The Security Council deals with matters of international peace and security. The Trusteeship Council was established to oversee the administration of trust territories but has suspended its operations since all trust territories have achieved self-government. The Economic and Social Council (ECOSOC) works on economic and social issues, the final approval of the UN's financial matters is the responsibility of the General Assembly.

topics.title: International Organisations II- League of Nations, UNO/ UN, OPEC, The Commonwealth of Nations

subject: Government
q: Power differs from influence in that it is
explanation:
Power differs from influence in that it is coercive while influence is persuasive.

Influence can be defined as the ability to create an impact on the beliefs and actions of an individual. Influence evokes respect. Those under the influence keep working in the desired manner even in the absence of the influential person. 

power is the ability of a person or a group of persons to enforce his or their decisions on other people by use of sanctions, punishment, or penalties against disobedience. On the other hand, through persuasion and conviction, an individual could be made to accept and obey the views of other persons.

subject: Government
q: The standing committee of a legislature is one
explanation:
A standing committee is a permanent committee within a legislature that is tasked with specific responsibilities defined by law, referred to as statutory responsibilities. These committees handle ongoing matters like reviewing bills, conducting hearings and making recommendations to the full legislature.

Ad hoc committees are temporary and created for specific tasks, while standing committees are permanent and handle ongoing duties.

subject: Government
q: Where the constitution is supreme, unconstitutional acts of the executive and the legislature can be checked by the courts through
explanation:
Where the constitution is supreme, unconstitutional acts of the executive and the legislature can be checked by the courts through judicial review.
 Judicial Review is the judicial power to declare any actions of the executive and the legislature unconstitutional, null and void.

subject: Government
q: The central decision-making organ of a confederation is made up of
options: politicians elected from the confederal constituencies
politicians nominated by the government of member states
explanation: The central decision-making organ of a confederation is made up of politicians nominated by governments of member states.

topics.title: The Workings of Government I - Organs & Systems

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is true of a parliamentary system of government?
explanation:
Adherence to majority rule is true of a parliamentary system of government.

This is so due to the fact that the party having a majority in the legislature controls both the executive and the legislature from where the Prime Minister and ministers of his cabinet are chosen. The stability of this system also depends upon the ruling party controlling a reasonable majority in the parliament.

topics.title: The Workings of Government I - Organs & Systems

subject: Government
q: A major feature of authoritarianism is that government is
explanation: A major feature of authoritarianism is that government is centralized. It should be remembered that authoritarianism is a form of government in which the state controls or directs everything.

subject: Government
q: The central point of capitalism, as expounded by Karl Marx, is that
options: capitalist profit is the surplus value obtained from workers labour
workers are inherently incapable of being owners of their labour
capitalists shall always increase worker earning capacity through wages
capitslists shall always readily consent to workers welfare demands.
explanation:
The central point of capitalism as expounded by Kari Marx is that capitalists' profit is the surplus value obtained from workers' labour.

According to Marx, workers produce more than what they get from employers. The capitalist employers buy the services of labour cheaply, but they sell the goods produced by labour at a rate higher than the amount spent for the payment of wages and the maintenance of the factory. They appropriate this excess as profit.

subject: Government
q: A constitution that requires a plebiscite or a referendum to be amended is
explanation:
Plebiscite; the direct vote of all the members of an electorate on an important public question such as a change in the constitution.

A referendum; is a direct and universal vote in which an entire electorate is invited to vote on a particular proposal and can have nationwide or local forms.

A rigid Constitution;  is a constitution that is difficult or slow to change usually because of a prescribed process of amendment that is detailed and lengthy in execution.

subject: Government
q: An important function of a constitution is that it
options: provide a framework  for the study of government
facilities cross-fertilization of ideas of governance
serves as the fountain head of authority for the exercise of power
promotes citizen participation in government and administration.
explanation: An important function of a constitution is that it serves as the fountain head of authority for the exercise of power, Constitutions prescribe how the power of the sate is to be allocated between the various organs of government. Most constitutions define the limits of a government's authority thereby establishing the various rights of a state's citizens.

subject: Government
q: when a bill passed by the legislature is vetoed by the executive, the action underscores the principle
explanation:
When a bill passed by the legislature is vetoed by the executive the action underscores the principle of checks and balances.

The doctrine or principle of checks and balances advocates the use of one organ of government to check the activities of the other organs. When the executive vetoes the bill passed by the legislature, and the executive has checked the activities of the legislature, thereby putting the principles of the checks and balances into practice.

subject: Government
q: In the legislative process, a bill is a
explanation: A bill is a formal proposal for new legislation or an amendment to existing legislation. It is introduced and presented for consideration by a legislative body, such as a parliament or congress, with the goal of becoming law if approved through the legislative process.

subject: Government
q: One of the advantages of a bicameral over a unicameral legislature is that it
options: takes less time for the bills to be passed
prevents the passage of ill-considered bills.
explanation:
One of the advantages of a bicameral over a unicameral legislature is that it prevents the passage of ill-considered bills.

According to J.C. Anyaele (1991) the second Chamber checks and prevents hasty and ill-considered passage of bills as done in a unicameral legislature.

topics.title: The Workings of Government I - Organs & Systems

subject: Government
q: The fundamental rights of citizens include rights to
options: free education, employment and freedom of thought
explanation:
The fundamental rights of citizens include rights to life,speech and association.

J. U. Anyaele (1991) enumerated some rights such as:

1. Right to life which no body shall intentionally deprive a citizen.

2. Right to peaceful assembly and association.

3. Right to freedom of expression and press.

other rights include;

  1. The right to dignity of person
  2. The right to fair hearing
  3. The right to freedom of movement
  4. The right from freedom of discrimination
  5. The right to personal liberty
  6. The right to vote and be voted for during an election
  7. The right to private family life
  8. Right to ownership of properties

subject: Government
q: The manipulation of boundaries of constituencies in order to win more seats is called
explanation:
The manipulation of boundaries of constituencies in order to win more seats is called gerrymandering.

According to Ofoegbu (1999) Gerry-mandering constituencies is the process of deliminating constituencies to favour a particular political party or candidate in the election.

subject: Government
q: One argument against a multi-party system is the
explanation: In a multi-party system, the presence of many political parties can lead to increased opposition and political instability, as parties may constantly oppose one another and create fragmentation within the political landscape. This can make it difficult to form strong, stable governments and may lead to frequent changes in leadership, which could hinder effective governance.

subject: Government
q: Associational interest groups are organized to
explanation:
Associational interest groups are organized to further the interests of members.


Pressure Groups, or interest groups, are defined as organizations or associations of people who seek to influence government policies and actions and people in authority positions to the benefit of their members. It could also be defined as an organized social interest group formed with the main aim of pursuing its interest and influencing government policies to the benefit of its members.

subject: Government
q: Public opinion is a view that is
explanation:
Public opinion is a view that is held by the majority. As far as public opinion is concerned, the view must be held and expressed by the majority of the society.

According to G. A. I. Ofoegbu (1999), public opinion is the believes, values and attitudes which are commonly held and expressed by majority of the people on a given issue.

subject: Government
q: The political neutrality of civil servants implies that they
options: are not allowed to join any organisation or group
are not allowed to be involved in partisan politics
explanation:
The political neutrality of civil servants implies that they are not allowed to be involved in partisan politics.


Political Neutrality is one of the main characteristics of Civil Service. This means that civil servants are not involved in partisan politics. They are there to serve any government that comes into power .

subject: Government
q: The idea of making the civil service permanent, neutral and anonymous is to
options: make civil servants a functional elite.
explanation:
The idea of making the civil service permanent, neutral and anonymous is to enhance efficiency in administration.

When a civil servant is made to enjoy secured appointment, he must definitely stand good chance to have good measure of expert knowledge and intelligence. When he is not neutral, his political views will definitely interfere with his official work. This will eventually create no conducive atmosphere for efficiency.

subject: Government
q: The western zone of the Sokoto caliphate was administered from
explanation: The Western zone of the Sokoto caliphate was administered from Gwandu - Othman Dan Fodio divided the Fulani empire into two- the Eastern and Western sections. The Eastern section included Kano, Katsina, Zaria, Bauchi, Gombe and Yola with Sokoto as its capital while Ilorin, Kontagoro and Argungu with Gwandu as its capital were included in the Western section.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: Some pre-colonial Nigerian societies are described as stateless because
options: they had no formal governmental institutions
they had no definite political boundaries
explanation:
Some pre-colonial Nigerian societies are described as stateless because they had no formal governmental institutions.
For instance, the Igbos unlike Yorubas and the Fulanis have a complex and complicated system of administration in pre-colonial era. There was no sort of central authority, what they had was a diffusion of political authority into different groups.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The method used by the British to facilitate the administration of Southern Nigeria was
explanation:
The method used by the British to facilitate the administration of Southern Nigeria was divide and rule.
The system brought about division between the traditional rulers who were looked upon a British puppets and the alienated educated elites thereby, strengthening the policy divide and rule.

subject: Government
q: A major function of the warrant chiefs was to
explanation:
A major function of the warrant Chiefs was to take charge of local administration.

The chiefs were the heads of the administration of their various communities.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: After 1945, the demand of African nationalists changed from reform to independence because
options: the second world war boosted their morale
the second world war enhanced colonial rule.
explanation:
After 1945, the demand of African nationalists changed from reform to independence because the second World War boosted their morale.

The war exposed the myth surrounding white supremacy. The ex-soldiers from West African saw that the whites were not as strong as they portrayed themselves to Nigerians and in fact that coming back from the war they assorted their rights and joined the nationalists in the struggle for independence.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: When Nigeria achieved independence in 1960, the head of state was the
explanation:
When Nigeria achieved independence in 1960, the Head of state was the Governor-General, representing the queen of England.
One of the main provisions of the constitution of 1960 was that the governor general was made the constitutional ceremonial Head of state and a representative of the Queen of England.

This means the queen was the head of state but appointed a representative in the person of the governor-general to stand in for her, in her absence.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Constitutional Development in Nigeria II - Independence & Post-Independence

subject: Government
q: The central legislature of Nigeria became bicameral in
explanation: The central legislature of Nigeria became bicameral in 1960.  Macpherson Constitution of 1951 created two chambers of regional legislatures known as the House of Chiefs and the House of Assembly in the Northern and Western regions. Coming then to the independence constitution of 1960, bicameral legislatures were adopted in both the federal and regional government including the Eastern region.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Constitutional Development in Nigeria II - Independence & Post-Independence

subject: Government
q: Which of these constitutions recognized local government as a third tier of government in Nigeria?
explanation: The 1979 constitutions recognized local government as a third tier government in Nigeria. General Murtala Mahammed inaugurated a Constitution Drafting Committee (CDC)on October 18, 1975. The draft constitution was then approved by General Olusegun Obasanjo as the 1979 Nigeria cobnstitution which took effect from October 1, 1979. One of the features of the constitution is that it provided for democratically elected local government councils as the third tier of government in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: Under the 1963 constitution, items not listed in the exclusive and concurrent list were within the exclusive competence of the
explanation:
Under the 1963 constitution, items not listed in the exclusive and concurrent list were within the exclusive competence of the regional legislatures.Sir Oliver Lyttleton constitution of 1954 classified government powers into three:
1. Exclusive powers - matters where only the federal or central government can legislate only.
2. Concurrent powers - matters where both the federal government and regional (state) can legislate and
Residual 0powers reserved for the regional legislature only.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Constitutional Development in Nigeria II - Independence & Post-Independence

subject: Government
q: Before Nigeria became a republic, the highest body charged with the administrator of justice was the
explanation: Before Nigeria became a republic, the highest body charged with the administration of justice was the Privy council. one of the features of the Republican Constitution of 1963 is that the supreme court of Nigeria became the highest court in Nigeria. Appeal cases stopped going to the privy council in London

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Constitutional Development in Nigeria II - Independence & Post-Independence

subject: Government
q: The equivalent of the commissioner at the local government level is the
explanation: The equivalent of a commissioner at the local government level is the supervisory Councillor. At the state level, a commissioner is the highest or completely in-charge of a particular ministry. For instance, the commissioner for education. He does not come in through election but through the appointment by the government of the state. Coming to the local government level, the supervisory Councillor for education as well as any other supervisory Councillor is appointed to the Executive Chairman of the local government subject to the approved of the Councillors.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The Independent National Electoral Commission has the power to prepare and maintain the register of
explanation: The Independent National Electoral Commission has the power to prepare and maintain the register of voters. One of functions of the body is registration of voters, compilation of voters register and updating of same from time to time.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Institutions of Government in Post-Independence Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The president of Nigeria is advised on the sovereignty and territorial integrity of the country by the
explanation: The powers of the President with respect to national security, both internal and external, are enormous as he is in complete control of the matter. He is the chairman of both the National Security Council and the National Defense Council and he appoints their members except the ex official members. The totality of the role of the National Defense Council is “to advise the President on matters relating to the defense of the sovereignty and territorial integrity of Nigeria”

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The Nigerian Youth Movement collapsed as the result of
options: shortage of funds to run its affairs
the harassment of its leadership by government.
explanation: The Nigerian Youth Movement collapsed as the result of the breakup of its leadership. The movement was in no distant time torn apart by leadership crisis. The crisis split the movement into factions. the two principal contestant were Ernest Ikoli and S.A Akorisanya

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The first restructure of the Nigerian Federation took place with the
explanation:
The first restructure of the Nigerian Federation took place with the creation of mid-west Region in 1963.

The creation of mid-west Region which came into effect on October 1, 1963 was one of the provisions of 1963 constitution. With that the mid-western Region was carried out from the western Region. Nigeria then had four Regions and no more three.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The land use decree of 1978 vested the ownership of land in Nigeria in the
explanation: The Land Use Decree of 1978 (later revised as the Land Use Act) vested the ownership of land in Nigeria's state governments. According to the decree, all land in urban and rural areas is held in trust by the state governors, who are responsible for managing it on behalf of the citizens. The goal was to ensure better land administration and allocation across the country.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The main source of financing local government in Nigeria is
explanation: In Nigeria, the main source of financing for local governments is statutory revenue allocation. This refers to the funds allocated to local governments by the federal government, which come from a share of the country's national revenue. These allocations are distributed based on the principles set out in the Nigerian Constitution and the revenue-sharing formula.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The most remarkable legacy of the 1976 Local Government Reform in Nigeria was the introduction of
explanation:
The most remarkable legacy of the 1976 Local Government Reform in Nigeria was the introduction of uniformity in structure and functions.

In February 1976 Federal Military Government issued a degree establishing a uniform Local Government Structure throughout the country. The measure is significant in a away in the sense that the first time in the history of Nigeria a national rather than Region/state approach was adopted toward Local Government.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The Murtala/Obasanjo regime in Nigeria increased the number of states from
explanation: The Murtala/Obasanjo regime in Nigeria increased the number of states from 12 to 19.Gowon created 12 states but the Murtala/Obasanjo regime created additional seven states bring the total number to 19

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The major factor that influenced the formulation of Nigerian foreign policy in the First Republic was
explanation:
The major factor that influenced the formulation of Nigerian foreign policy in the First Republic was the colonial legacy.

According to J.U. Anyaele (1994), we can rightly agree that the colonial legacy fore-ordained our foreign policy initially. This is one of the major reasons why the Balewa Administration maintains close relations mainly with Britain and other commonwealth countries.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community

subject: Government
q: The Barclay's Bank and the British Petroleum Company in Nigeria were nationalised in the late 1970's for transacting business with
explanation:
The Barclays Bank and the British Petroleum Company in Nigeria were nationalized in the late 1970s for transacting business with South Africa.

It was Obasanjo's Administration that chased Britain's arrogance over Zimbabwe's Independence.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community

subject: Government
q: South-South cooperation is a major policy plan on which Nigeria bases her relations with
explanation: South-South cooperation refers to the collaboration between countries in the Global South, primarily focused on mutual development, technical exchange and sharing of resources and knowledge to address common challenges. Nigeria, as a developing country, engages in South-South cooperation with other developing nations, rather than with developed countries.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community

subject: Government
q: Nigeria's recognition of the Popular Movement for the Liberation of Angola to lead that country was made under
explanation:
Nigeria's recognition of the popular movement for the liberation of Angola to lead that country was made under General Murtala Muhammed.

According to J. U Anyaele (1994) Martala's administration's recognition of Agostintion Neto's popular movement for the liberation of Angola (MPLA) at the expenses of us and South Africa's support of UNITA and FNLA played a decisive role in Angolan independence"

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community

subject: Government
q: The first Nigerian Permanent Representative to the United Nations was
explanation: The first Nigerian Permanent Representative to the United Nations is Jaja Wachukwu but the first among the options provided is Samuel Adebo.

subject: Government
q: In 1981, Nigeria participated in an Organization of African Unity peace-keeping force to replace Libyan forces in
explanation:
In 1981, Nigeria participated in an Organization of African Unity peace-keeping force to replace Libyan forces in Chad. 

Five African nations formally agreed to the the deployment of peacekeeping troops in Chad. the first such force assembled by the Organization of African Unity.

President Goukouni Oueddei of Chad met with Kenya's President, Daniel arap Moi, and officials from four of the six African nations that have pledged to contribute troops to the force. The O.A.U. soldiers will replace Libyan troops that have withdrawn.

subject: Government
q: During the Civil War, the major power that expressed moral support for Biafra's self-determination was
explanation:
During the Civil War, the major power that expressed moral support for Biafra's self determination was France.

France gave both financial and material assistance to Biafra.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: Nigeria's withdrawal from the Edinburgh Commonwealth Games in July 1986 was in protest against British
options: support for UNITA rebels in Angola
failure to impose sanctions on South Africa
explanation:
Nigeria's withdrawal from the Edinburgh Commonwealth Games in July 1986 was in protest against British failure to impose sanctions on South Africa.


She, Britain did not support but in Apartheid regime in South Africa.

q: The major demand of the Third World countries on the United Nations in recent times is the
options: expansion of the permanent membership of the security Council
post of the Secretary-General of the organization
withdrawal of the veto power from the security council
enforcement of resolutions on the super powers
explanation:
The major demand of the Third World countries on the United Nations in recent times is the expansion of the permanent membership of the Security Council.

One of the achievements of the O.A.U. is provision of one voice. As a result of now able to speak with one at the United Nations General Assembly as well as its other organs such as the Security

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is the function of the Council of the Ministers of the O.A.U
options: Cordinating of the general policy of the organization
Directing the finances of the organization
Preparing the agenda of the organization's meetings
Reviewing the functions and activities of other organs of the organizations
explanation:
Preparing the agenda of the organisation's meeting is the function of the council of Ministers of the O.A.U.

The Council of Ministers is the body which is composed of the foreign ministers of member countries. it meets about twice a year but extra-ordinary meetings may be called if and such a request is approved by two-thirds of the members.

One of the responsibilities is preparing the agenda for the summit i.e the Assembly of Heads of State and Government
