subject: Government
q: Price stability in the international oil market is the major objective of the
explanation: The major objective of the Organisation of Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) is to ensure price stability in the international oil market. This is to ensure that fluctuation is eliminated and that every member-state can benefit from oil sales.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations II- League of Nations, UNO/ UN, OPEC, The Commonwealth of Nations

subject: Government
q: Nigeria played fundamental roles in the creation of
explanation:
The Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) was created on the 28th of May, 1975 in Lagos, Nigeria under the leadership of General Yakubu Gowon of Nigeria and President Eyedama of Togo. 

The organisation is headquartered in Abuja, Nigeria whereas its financial headquarters is in Lome, Togo.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations I - OAU, AU, ECOWAS, APPO

subject: Government
q: The most effective instrument adopted by Nigeria against the East-West Cold War in the 1960s was
explanation: During the 1960s, Nigeria adopted the non-alignment policy as an effective instrument to navigate the East-West Cold War. This policy meant that Nigeria chose not to align with either the capitalist bloc (led by the United States) or the communist bloc (led by the Soviet Union). Instead, it focused on maintaining independence in foreign relations and avoiding entanglement in ideological conflicts between the two superpowers.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community

subject: Government
q: The bipolar world was a major factor for the formation of
explanation: The Non-Aligned Movement (NAM) is a forum of 120 countries that are not formally aligned with or against any major power bloc. It was founded with the view of advancing interests of developing countries in the context of Cold War confrontation.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community

subject: Government
q: The June 12 election in Nigeria was annulled by the regime of
explanation: The Presidential Election of 1993 which was supposedly meant to transfer power back to civilian rule from the hands of Ibrahim Babangida was contested by Alhaji Tofa of the NRC and Moshood K.O. Abiola of the SDP. The Armed Forces Ruling Council (AFRC) then annulled the entire exercise.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: Military intervention in Nigerian politics was first witnessed in
explanation:
Military intervention was first witnessed on the 25th of January, 1966 when General Aguiyi Ironsi took power from the then Prime Minister, Alhaji Tafawa Balewa. He immediately suspended the constitution and established a unitary system of government. 

The military claimed that the reason for its intervention was to quell the ethnic tension in the country as well as stop corruption.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: A major problem affecting Local government in Nigeria today is
explanation:
State government interference is one of the major problems affecting local governments in Nigeria today. Local governments are supposed to operate as autonomous entities under the Nigerian constitution, but state governments often exert undue control. This includes:

1. They have control over local government finances through the State Joint Local Government Account. 

2. They interfere in the decision making of local government officials and impose administrative staff.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: Public corporations can be controlled by
explanation: The courts can declare any act made by some corporations illegal or unconstitutional. This is done when they are breaching the Act upon which they are set up.

subject: Government
q: Public corporations in Nigeria are established through
explanation: Public Corporations are established or brought into being through an act of parliament.

subject: Government
q: A major instrument designed to promote the workings of Nigerian federalism is
explanation: The Federal Character Commission (FCC) is a major instrument designed to ensure fairness and equity in the distribution of public offices, resources and socio-economic opportunities across Nigeria's diverse regions. The FCC works to prevent dominance by any single group or region.

subject: Government
q: The party that controlled the government of South West States after the 1979 General Elections was
explanation: The Unity Party of Nigeria, founded and led by Chief Obafemi Awolowo won the governorship election in 5 states - Lagos Ondo, Oyo, Ogun State and Bendel thus controlling the government of the South West states.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: Recruitment and promotion of civil servants in Nigeria remain the responsibility of
explanation: The Civil Service Commission is an independent body responsible for the recruitment and promotion of civil servants.

subject: Government
q: The commission that conducted elections that ushered in the Fourth Republic in Nigeria is
explanation: The 1999 constitution assigns the power to conduct elections to the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC). It is this body that conducted the election that ushered in the Fourth Republic.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Constitutional Development in Nigeria II - Independence & Post-Independence

subject: Government
q: Before a treaty can be binding on a country, it has to be approved by the
explanation: One of the functions of the legislature is to ratify or approve the treaty that has been signed by an ambassador to the country. It can only be binding on a nation if it has been approved by the legislature.

topics.title: The Workings of Government I - Organs & Systems

subject: Government
q: The first constitution to introduce a presidential system was the
explanation: The constitution that introduced the Presidential system in Nigeria is the 1979 Constitution. Before 1979, Nigeria's civilian rule was characterised by a Parliamentary system where the powers of hthe ead of state and head of government are divided. In the First Republic (1963-66), the Head of State was Nnamdi Azikwe while the head of government was Prime Minister Alhaji Tafawa Balewa. However, in 1979, Nigeria chose to adopt the Presidential System just like the United States of America. The first executive president in Nigeria was Shehu Shagari.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Constitutional Development in Nigeria II - Independence & Post-Independence

subject: Government
q: One of the internal factors that led to the rise and growth of nationalism in Nigeria was
explanation: Some Africans fought alongside Britain and the allied forces during the Second World War against Germany and the Axis powers. The job promised them after the war was not given to them on their return to West Africa. This is one of the internal factors that led to the rise and growth of nationalism.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The political subjugation and economic exploitation of a people is known as
explanation: Colonialism is a form of government where a dominant country enforces its political will on a country in order to exploit its economic resources.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Colonial Administration I - British Colonial Administration

subject: Government
q: The leader of the Oyo Mesi in the Yoruba pre-colonial political system was
explanation:
The Bashorun is the leader of the Oyo Mesi. He is the Prime Minister of the Oyo  Empire and his responsibilities include:

1. To implement the decisions made by the Alaafin and the Oyo Mesi.

2. To represent the voice of the Oyo Mesi and the people.

3. To present, on behalf of the Oyo Mesi, the empty calabash, to the Alaafin as a representation of a vote of no confidence in the Alaafin When this is done, the Alaafin is either meant to commit ritual suicide or to go on exile.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: Strike action is mostly used by
explanation: One of the modes of operation of pressure groups is strike action. Members of a pressure group can choose not to show up to work and instead engage in protests in order to pressure or influence the government to create certain policies that suit their interests.

subject: Government
q: One of the functions of political party is
explanation: One of the functions of political parties is to educate the public on political issues. They do not promote their interest but rather aggregate the interests of the common people. The organisation of elections and the announcement of election results are the duty of the electoral commission.

subject: Government
q: The right to vote and be voted for is
explanation: Franchise is the right of qualified adult citizens to vote and be voted for.

subject: Government
q: The first past the post system of voting is also referred to as
explanation: The First-Past-The-Post (FPTP) system is a common electoral system where the candidate who receives the most votes wins, even if they do not receive an absolute majority (more than 50%). "Simple majority" refers to having more votes than any other single candidate.

subject: Government
q: A community that cooperates and harnesses its resources together is said to be practising
explanation: The term communalism is a social or economic system where members of a community work together for their mutual benefit. This is an ideology and mode of production where a particular community collectively owns, manages and controls resources and the means of production. In this system, members live close together, support one another and pool their resources to pursue common goals.

subject: Government
q: The African leaders formally marked the end of the Organisation of African Unity and replaced it with the African Union in
explanation: On this date, the African Union (AU) was officially launched at the Durban Summit in South Africa, formally replacing the Organisation of African Unity (OAU), which had been in existence since 1963.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations I - OAU, AU, ECOWAS, APPO

subject: Government
q: A permanent structure that facilitates continuity in governance is the
explanation:
The civil service is the permanent administrative arm of the government, composed of non-elected, career officials who remain in office regardless of changes in political leadership.

The civil service has several key features that ensure a government continues to function smoothly even when political leaders change:

  • Permanence: Unlike elected officials (the political class) whose tenure is limited by elections, civil servants have permanent careers and job security.

  • Institutional Memory: Because they serve for long periods, civil servants preserve the "memory" of how government programs operate to provide stability during political transitions.

  • Political Neutrality: Civil servants are expected to be non-partisan, serving whichever government is in power with equal dedication to ensure no breakdown in state activities.

  • Policy Implementation: While politicians formulate policies, the civil service is the "engine room" that actually translates those policies into concrete actions and public services.

subject: Government
q: The two constitutions that provided for the election of four legislative members were
explanation:
The Clifford Constitution introduced the elective principle for the first time. It provided for 4 elected members: 3 for Lagos and 1 for Calabar.Richard Constitution1946

The Richards Constitution retained the elective principle from the 1922 constitution. It continued to provide for 4 elected members: 3 for Lagos and 1 for Calabar.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Constitutional Development in Nigeria I - Pre-Independence

subject: Government
q: One of the functions of Public Corporation is to
explanation:
The primary purpose of a public corporation is to handle services that are vital to the public welfare but may be too capital-intensive for private individuals. They are set up to provide necessities such as water, electricity and telecommunications at a reduced cost to the public.

Unlike private businesses, their primary aim is not profit-making, but rather the social and economic development of the country.

subject: Government
q: The appointments of qualified personnel into any federal establishment as a minister were to reflect the principle of
explanation:
In Nigeria, the appointment of ministers must reflect the Federal Character principle to ensure balanced representation from across the country.

The Nigerian Constitution mandates that the President must appoint at least one minister from each state of the federation to satisfy this requirement. This principle was introduced in the 1979 Constitution to prevent any single ethnic or regional group from dominating the government and to promote national unity.

 The Federal Character Commission was established to monitor and enforce this fair distribution of political and bureaucratic positions.

subject: Government
q: The pioneer president of the Action Group was
explanation:
Chief Obafemi Awolowo was the founder and first leader of the Action Group (AG), which was established in March 1951. The party grew out of a cultural organisation called Egbe Omo Oduduwa and became a major political force in the Western Region.

Awolowo led the party through the pre-independence era and served as the first Premier of the Western Region starting in 1954.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: Nigeria was a member of
explanation: Nigeria is a member of the Organisation of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC), having joined in 1971. Nigeria is NOT a member of the ARAB League, EAC (East African Community) or NATO.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations I - OAU, AU, ECOWAS, APPO

subject: Government
q: The Nigerian Supreme Court became the highest court of appeal in the
explanation: The 1963 Republican Constitution established the Supreme Court of Nigeria as the highest court of appeal. This abolished the previous system where appeals went to the Judicial Committee of the Privy Council in the United Kingdom. This marked Nigeria's full judicial sovereignty.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Constitutional Development in Nigeria II - Independence & Post-Independence

subject: Government
q: One of the major reasons for colonialism in Africa was
explanation:
A major driving force for European colonialism in Africa was economic interest, including the pursuit of raw materials, new markets and the exploitation of natural resources like gold and diamonds.

While religious and humanitarian factors were used as public justifications, the primary motivation was the material greed and material exploitation of the continent.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Colonial Administration III - Putting it all together...

subject: Government
q: One major feature of Nigerian foreign policy is
options: non-alignment with any of the power blocs
explanation: A major feature of Nigerian foreign policy since independence is non-alignment. This means Nigeria chose not to formally ally itself with either the Western (USA) or Eastern (Soviet) blocs during the Cold War, maintaining independence in its international relations.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community

subject: Government
q: Which of these is a professional pressure group in Nigeria?
explanation:
The Nigerian Union of Teachers (NUT) is a professional pressure group because it represents the specific interests of people in the teaching profession.

  • NLC: Is the central labour organisation 

  • MAN: Represents manufacturers/employers.

  • Arewa Consultative Forum: Is a socio-cultural and political group.

subject: Government
q: Local Government Areas in Nigeria are by act of the 1999 constitution a/ an
explanation:
Section 7 of the 1999 Constitution guarantees the existence of local government councils as the third tier of government. The Constitution prescribes their structure, finance and functions, aiming for them to be responsive and accountable to citizens at the grassroots level.

While they are theoretically autonomous, in practice they struggle with financial independence because of the State Joint Local Government Account (SJLGA).

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The fusion of executive and legislative powers is a core feature of
explanation: In a parliamentary system, the executive branch (the Prime Minister and Cabinet) is drawn directly from the legislative branch (Parliament). This creates a "fusion of powers" rather than a strict separation.

topics.title: The Workings of Government I - Organs & Systems

subject: Government
q: A proposal discussed in the parliament and passed for signing to become a law is a
explanation: A bill is a draft or a formal proposal for a new law (or a change to an existing one) that is introduced and discussed in a legislature, such as a parliament. It only becomes an act (law) after it has been passed by the legislature and received the required signature or assent from the head of state.

subject: Government
q: The highest court in the Hausa-Fulani political system was presided by
explanation: In the pre-colonial Hausa-Fulani political system, the Emir held supreme executive, legislative and judicial powers. While minor cases were handled in local Alkali courts presided over by trained Islamic judges, the Emir's Court served as the highest court in the land and the final court of appeal.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The New Partnership for Africa's Development (NEPAD) was established in
explanation: The New Partnership for Africa's Development (NEPAD) was officially adopted by African Heads of State and Government in July 2001 during a summit in Lusaka, Zambia. It was formed through the merger of two existing plans: the Millennium Partnership for Africa's Recovery Programme (MAP) and the OMEGA Plan.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations I - OAU, AU, ECOWAS, APPO

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is NOT a duty of citizens in a state?
explanation:
Spying on the state is not a duty; it is considered an act of treason or a criminal offence against the state. Common duties and responsibilities of citizens include:

  • Payment of taxes: Promptly declaring income and paying required levies to fund government services.

  • Voting at elections: Participating in the selection of political leaders.

  • Prevention of crimes: Reporting suspected criminal activities to the appropriate authorities.

subject: Government
q: Confederalism could be regarded as
explanation: In a confederal system (or confederation), power is highly decentralised. The central government is weak, limited to coordinating common interests like defence or foreign affairs, while the component units (states) remain strong and retain their individual sovereignty.

subject: Government
q: When a constitution requires a special organ or a more difficult amendment procedure, such constitution is applicable in which system of government?
explanation: When a constitution requires a "special organ" or a "more difficult amendment procedure," it is known as a rigid constitution. This is a hallmark of a federal system of government, where the constitution is supreme and designed to protect the division of power between the central government and the constituent states.

subject: Government
q: The NPN presidential candidate in the Second Republic was
explanation: During Nigeria's Second Republic, Alhaji Shehu Shagari was the presidential candidate for the National Party of Nigeria (NPN). He won the 1979 election and became the first executive president of Nigeria.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The detection, prevention and investigation of criminal activities in Nigeria is the primary responsibility of the
explanation: In Nigeria, the Police (specifically the Nigeria Police Force) have the primary constitutional responsibility for the detection, prevention and investigation of criminal activities. While citizens and vigilantes can assist, the formal legal authority for investigation lies with the police.

subject: Government
q: The essence of the doctrine of separation of powers is to prevent
explanation: The primary goal of separating powers into Executive, Legislative and Judicial branches is to create checks and balances, ensuring that no single branch becomes too powerful or tyrannical.

subject: Government
q: The determinants of a country's foreign policy are often influenced by
explanation: While many factors play a role, economic interests, such as trade agreements and resource access, are central to how a country interacts with others and shapes its foreign policy.

subject: Government
q: The system of government in which the central  and component units possess coordinated powers is
explanation: In a Federal system, power is constitutionally divided and shared between a central (national) government and various component units (like states or provinces). The term "coordinated powers" refers to the fact that both levels of government are supreme within their own spheres of influence and must work in tandem.

topics.title: The Workings of Government II - Unitary, Federal & Confederal Structures

subject: Government
q: The primary function of the judiciary is to
explanation: In a standard democratic system governed by the separation of powers, each branch of government has a distinct and primary role. The judiciary is tasked with explaining and clarifying the meaning of laws passed by the legislature.

topics.title: The Workings of Government I - Organs & Systems

subject: Government
q: The Commonwealth Games were last hosted by Nigeria in
options: Nigeria has never hosted the commonwealth games
explanation: Nigeria has never hosted the Commonwealth Games.

subject: Government
q: An organised group of citizens acting together and seeking to obtain the control of government is a
explanation: A political party is defined as an organised group of citizens who share similar political aims and seek to obtain control of the government by getting their candidates elected to public office.

subject: Government
q: Democracy is a system in which the
explanation: Democracy is a system based on the principle of majority rule, where political decisions are made through voting. In a healthy democracy, this is balanced with minority rights to ensure that smaller groups are protected from "the tyranny of the majority".

subject: Government
q: An important characteristic of state is
explanation: Sovereignty is considered one of the most important and essential characteristics of a state. It refers to the state's supreme authority over its territory and its independence from external control. Other essential elements of a state include a defined territory, a population, and a legitimate government.

subject: Government
q: The authority of a leader derived from a constitution is
explanation: This refers to authority derived from a system of laws and a constitution, rather than from personal charisma or ancient tradition.

subject: Government
q: Census controversy was one of the factors that contributed to the military intervention in
explanation: The census controversy (specifically the disputed 1963 census) was one of the major factors—alongside widespread electoral malpractice in the Western Region—that created the political instability leading to Nigeria's first military intervention on January 15, 1966.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The African Union came into existence in 2002 when it replaced the
explanation: The AU officially replaced the OAU (established in 1963) in July 2002 at the Durban summit.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations I - OAU, AU, ECOWAS, APPO

subject: Government
q: To ensure political neutrality of civil service, members must
explanation: To ensure they remain impartial and serve any government regardless of the party in power, civil servants must avoid active involvement in political parties.

subject: Government
q: Public opinion allows an organised government to
options: infringe the rights of its opponents
find out what the public thinks of its activities
explanation: Public opinion allows governments to obtain input and communication from the people they represent.

subject: Government
q: The Nigeria Railway Corporation is an example of
explanation: The NRC is a state-owned enterprise with the exclusive right to operate railways in Nigeria.

subject: Government
q: The Head of the Army in the Yoruba political kingdom is
explanation: This title was given to the generalissimo or war general of the old Oyo Empire.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The longest and most expensive military transition programme in Nigeria was under
explanation: While General Yakubu Gowon's transition was prolonged, Babangida's transition program (1987–1993) is widely cited in political studies as the longest and most expensive in Nigeria's history due to its multiple postponements, creation of political parties by the government and the building of party secretariats in every local government area.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: Sovereignty refers to the
options: the supreme power of a state to use force in order to enforce obedience
the supreme power of a state to negotiate with other states
the supreme power of a state to govern other nations
the supreme power and authority of a state over its own affairs
explanation:
Sovereignty is the supreme power and authority of a state over its own affairs. This is the most accurate definition because it encompasses two key dimensions:

  1. Internal Sovereignty: The power of a state to make and enforce laws within its own borders without interference from internal groups or individuals.

  2. External Sovereignty: The right of a state to exist independently and manage its own foreign policy without being dictated to by other nations.

subject: Government
q: A major argument against the First Republic parties is
explanation:
The political parties of the First Republic (1960–1966) are most heavily criticised for being ethnically based and regionally focused. Instead of operating as national entities with a broad country-wide appeal, the three major parties were tied directly to specific regions and ethnic groups:

  • NPC (Northern People's Congress): Rooted in the Northern Region and primarily representing Hausa-Fulani interests.

  • NCNC (National Council of Nigerian Citizens): While initially more national, it became increasingly associated with the Eastern Region and the Igbo people.

  • AG (Action Group): Based in the Western Region and largely representing Yoruba interests.

This "tripartite federal structure" led to a "winner-takes-all" political culture where parties prioritised their own regions over national unity, ultimately resulting in the political instability that led to the 1966 coup.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: Public corporations were established by an act of
explanation: A public corporation is a government-owned enterprise established by a specific Act of Parliament (or a statute/decree) to provide essential services to the public. These corporations are "statutory bodies," meaning they are created by law. The Act of Parliament defines their specific powers, functions and duties

subject: Government
q: The most popular method employed by pressure groups is
explanation:
Lobbying is considered the most popular and major method used by pressure groups because it involves direct interaction with legislators and policymakers to influence decisions before they become law.  It can include sending letters, providing research data, making presentations, or face-to-face meetings with government officials.

While strikes are a powerful tool used by labour unions, they are a "method of last resort" when persuasion fails. Lobbying is the day-to-day, ongoing activity of most successful pressure groups.

subject: Government
q: The elective principle was first introduced under
explanation: The Clifford Constitution of 1922 was the first to introduce the elective principle in Nigeria. This led to the first-ever elections into the Legislative Council, with three seats for Lagos and one seat for Calabar. The introduction of this principle awakened political consciousness, encouraged the formation of the first political parties (such as the NNDP), and increased the tempo of nationalism.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Constitutional Development in Nigeria I - Pre-Independence

subject: Government
q: One of the key characteristics of the civil service is
explanation: Anonymity is a core principle of the civil service, meaning that civil servants should neither be blamed nor praised in public for the successes or failures of government policy. Other standard characteristics include permanence (stable employment), neutrality (staying apolitical), and impartiality (treating all citizens equally).

subject: Government
q: Laws made by the local government, public corporations and ministries is a
explanation: Laws enacted by local authorities, public corporations or professional bodies are called bye-laws. These are limited to the specific area or jurisdiction of the body that created them, such as a law passed by a city council regarding local markets.

subject: Government
q: The group that had the best egalitarian traditional political system is the
explanation: Unlike the centralised monarchies of the Yoruba or Hausa, the Igbo traditional political system was decentralised and "acephalous" (lacking a single central head). Power was exercised through a council of elders, age grades, and general assemblies by consensus, emphasising an egalitarian and "ultra-democratic" social structure.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: Nigeria applies soft diplomacy in Africa through
explanation: Nigeria applies soft diplomacy through the Nigerian Technical Aid Corps (TAC), which sends Nigerian professionals (doctors, engineers, teachers) to assist other African, Caribbean and Pacific countries. This programme aims to share Nigeria's human resources to foster development and strengthen diplomatic ties without using military or economic coercion.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community

subject: Government
q: The proto-nationalism before the Second World War was characterised by the
explanation:
Proto-nationalism refers to the early stage of nationalist movements in Africa before the World War II. During this period, nationalist activities were mainly led by:

  • Western-educated elites

  • Lawyers

  • Journalists

  • Teachers

  • Clergy

These educated elements used newspapers, petitions and constitutional agitation to demand reforms from the colonial government. In Nigeria, early nationalist figures like Herbert Macaulay and Nnamdi Azikiwe were part of this educated elite class.

subject: Government
q: The main objectives of African Union is to
options: regulate business transactions within the international community among members
promote co-operation and economic development  among members
oversee the free flow of political engagement in Africa and developed nations
settle disputes among South East Asia countries
explanation: The AU's primary goals are to achieve greater unity among African countries and to accelerate the political and socio-economic integration of the continent. It focuses on peace, security and stability as a foundation for economic development.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations I - OAU, AU, ECOWAS, APPO

subject: Government
q: A type of constitution where the procedure for its amendment is enshrined in it is a
explanation: A rigid constitution is one where the procedure for its amendment is difficult and enshrined within the document. Its amendment requires a special majority (e.g., two-thirds) in the legislature.

subject: Government
q: A form of government where the ultimate authority to govern is vested with the people is
explanation: Democracy is a system of government where the ultimate power is vested in the people, who exercise it directly or through freely elected representatives.

subject: Government
q: The right to vote and be voted in an election is
explanation: The right to vote and be voted for in an election is known as the franchise.

subject: Government
q: The main reason for military intervention in Nigerian politics is the
options: perceived incapability of civilians to govern
explanation: The primary reason cited for military intervention in Nigerian politics has been the perceived failure or incapacity of civilian governments to manage the economy, maintain order or curb corruption.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The coordination and unification of the petroleum policies of its members is one of the main objectives of
explanation:
OPEC stands for the Organisation of the Petroleum Exporting Countries. It was founded in 1960 with the specific goal of managing the supply of oil in an effort to set the price of oil on the world market.

The primary objectives of OPEC are:

  • Coordination and Unification: Aligning the petroleum policies of member countries to ensure stability in the oil market.

  • Price Stability: Preventing fluctuations that could be harmful to both producers and consumers.

  • Steady Income: Securing an efficient, economic, and regular supply of petroleum to consuming nations and a fair return on capital for those investing in the petroleum industry.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations II- League of Nations, UNO/ UN, OPEC, The Commonwealth of Nations

subject: Government
q: Which of the following factors can limit the application of the rule of law?
explanation:
The rule of law requires that justice be delivered fairly and within a reasonable timeframe. When courts are overwhelmed with a backlog of cases, it creates significant barriers to its application:

  • Delayed Justice: A core tenet of the rule of law is that "justice delayed is justice denied." High caseloads mean litigants may wait years for a hearing, making the legal system ineffective for resolving urgent disputes.

  • Loss of Public Confidence: Continuous delays and "nonsense" results from backlogs can erode the public's trust in the judiciary and the fairness of laws.

  • Weakened Enforcement: Large backlogs prevent the timely enforcement of rights, which directly weakens the democratic principle that everyone is subject to and protected by the law.

subject: Government
q: A system in which more than two political parties contest for an election is referred to as
explanation: In a multi-party system, more than two distinct political parties exist, are officially recognised, and regularly compete for and win public offices. This system provides voters with a wider range of choices compared to other systems.

subject: Government
q: The Independent National Electoral Commission is responsible for
explanation:
The Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) is responsible for (B) conducting general elections.

Key Functions of INEC:

  • Electoral Management: Its primary role is to organise, undertake, and supervise elections for the offices of the President, Governors, and members of the National and State Assemblies.

  • Voter Registration: It registers qualified voters and maintains the national register of voters.

  • Party Regulation: It registers political parties and monitors their organization and finances.

subject: Government
q: Public corporations are entities
explanation: Public corporations are government-owned establishments created through specific Acts of Parliament (statutes), decrees, or edicts. They are established to provide essential social and welfare services to the public, such as water and electricity. They are separate legal entities, meaning they can sue and be sued in their own name. Their primary goal is public service rather than making a profit.

subject: Government
q: The use of Prime Minister as the head of Government is associated with which system of governance?
explanation: The use of a Prime Minister as the head of government is the defining feature of a Parliamentary system (also known as the Westminster model). In this system, there is usually a distinction between the Head of State (a Monarch or President with ceremonial powers) and the Head of Government (the Prime Minister, who holds executive power).

subject: Government
q: The institution of government that interprets laws made by the legislature is
explanation:
Under the doctrine of the separation of powers, each branch has a specific role:

  • Legislature: Makes the laws.

  • Executive: Enforces or puts the laws into operation.

  • Judiciary: Interprets, defends and applies the law to specific cases.

topics.title: The Workings of Government I - Organs & Systems

subject: Government
q: Citizenship is best defined as a member of a state who is legally and constitutionally
explanation: Citizenship is best defined as a member of a state who is legally and constitutionally recognised as having the full rights and responsibilities of that state. Recognition by the law allows a person to enjoy civil and political rights, such as voting.

subject: Government
q: Fascism and Nazism are ideologies that were promoted respectively by
explanation: Fascism and Nazism were two far-right political ideologies that rose to prominence in Europe during the interwar period. Promoted by Benito Mussolini in Italy. He founded the National Fascist Party in 1921 and became the dictator of Italy. Nazism, short for National Socialism, was the German variant of fascism promoted by Adolf Hitler. While similar to Italian fascism in its authoritarianism, Nazism placed a much heavier emphasis on racial purity and the superiority of the "Aryan" race.

subject: Government
q: The process by which citizens get involved in the political activities and decision-making of their country is
explanation:
The process by which citizens get involved in the political activities and decision-making of their country is known as political participation. It describes a broad range of voluntary activities intended to influence public policy and the selection of political leaders. While voting is the most frequent form, political participation also includes working on campaigns, joining political parties, contacting public officials, protesting and petitioning.

It is considered vital for a healthy democracy because it ensures the government hears the voices of its citizens and remains accountable to them.

subject: Government
q: The age qualifications of members of the Senate and House of Representatives under the 1979 constitution were
explanation:
Under the 1979 Constitution of Nigeria, specific age requirements were set for those wishing to contest for seats in the National Assembly:

  • Senate: A candidate was required to have attained the age of 30 years.

  • House of Representatives: A candidate was required to have attained the age of 21 years.

These requirements changed in later constitutions (such as the 1999 Constitution), which raised the ages to 35 for the Senate and 30 for the House of Representatives.

subject: Government
q: The War Against Indiscipline campaign was launched by the military regime of
explanation:
The War Against Indiscipline (WAI) was a mass mobilisation program in Nigeria aimed at correcting social maladjustment and corruption. It was officially launched in March 1984. It was a hallmark initiative of the military government of Major General Muhammadu Buhari (1983–1985). The program was announced by his second-in-command, Brigadier General Tunde Idiagbon.

The campaign promoted orderliness, such as queuing correctly for buses, and instilled a strong work ethic and national unity. It also mandated the prominent display of the national flag and the singing of the national anthem in schools.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: A bicameral legislature is made up of
explanation: A bicameral legislature is a system of government in which the legislative branch is divided into two separate houses or chambers, such as a Senate and a House of Representatives.

subject: Government
q: Colonial policy of assimilation was introduced in Africa by the
explanation: The French colonial administration famously used the Policy of Assimilation, which aimed to turn African subjects into "Frenchmen" by teaching them French culture, language and laws.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Colonial Administration II - French Colonial Administration

subject: Government
q: A confederal system of governance is characterised by
explanation:
A confederal system (or confederation) is a form of governance where power is decentralised. In this system, sovereign states or independent regional governments delegate only limited, specific powers to a weak central authority.

 The regional governments retain their individual sovereignty and independence, often holding the right to secede from the confederation

subject: Government
q: Registration of political parties is the responsibility of the
explanation:
In most democratic systems, the Electoral Commission (such as INEC in Nigeria or the FEC in the US) is the independent body legally empowered to oversee the democratic process. Their responsibilities  include:

  • Registering political parties: Ensuring they meet legal requirements to exist.

  • Conducting elections: Organising the voting process and announcing results.

  • Voter registration: Maintaining the database of eligible voters.

  • Monitoring party finances: Ensuring parties follow laws regarding campaign funding.

subject: Government
q: Government can ascertain feedback on public policy through
explanation:
In a democratic system, governments use public opinion as a primary tool to understand how citizens feel about current laws and future policies. This feedback loop is essential because:

  • Measuring Effectiveness: Polling and surveys allow agencies to collect targeted data on the quality and impact of public services.

  • Direct Interaction: Public opinion is gauged through methods like citizen satisfaction surveys, town hall meetings, and public consultations, where citizens can voice concerns directly to government representatives.

  • Accountability: It serves as a check on those in power by elevating the concerns of everyday people.

subject: Government
q: Which of the following is not an obligation of a citizen?
explanation:
In a democracy, there is a clear distinction between obligations (things you must do) and rights (things you are permitted to do).

Contesting an election is considered a right or privilege (the "right to be voted for"). A citizen can choose to run for office, but they are not legally required to do so.

subject: Government
q: A sovereign state is one
options: which sovereignty is vested in the military
whose government decisions are made independent
where its citizens can speak without fear or favour
whose constitution could only be changed by the military government
explanation: A sovereign state has complete self-determination over its internal and external affairs, meaning it does not look beyond its own borders for the source of its legal authority. For a state to be truly sovereign, its government must function without foreign interference or control.

subject: Government
q: An election that allows political parties to choose a flag bearer for general election is called
explanation: A primary election is the process which party members or the public select a candidate to represent that party in a general election. Its purpose is to narrow the field of candidates and determine who will be the official "flag bearer" for the party

subject: Government
q: The British company granted the charter to rule and administer the northern area before direct colonial rule was known as
explanation: The Royal Niger Company was a British chartered company that was instrumental in spreading British influence in West Africa. It was granted a royal charter in 1886, giving it authority to rule and administer territories along the Niger River. In 1900, the company handed over its administrative control to the British Crown, leading to the creation of the Northern Nigeria Protectorate.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria
Colonial Administration I - British Colonial Administration

subject: Government
q: Nigeria became a federation of nineteen states in
explanation:
Nigeria’s state structure has evolved significantly since independence. Under the military regime of General Murtala Muhammed, the number of states was increased from 12 to 19. This was done to bring the government closer to the people and address ethnic tensions.

The 1976 decree created seven new states: Bauchi, Benue, Borno, Imo, Niger, Ogun, and Ondo. It also renamed several existing states (e.g., North-Western State became Sokoto and Niger).

It was also in 1976 that the decision was made to move the capital from Lagos to Abuja, designating it as the Federal Capital Territory (FCT).

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: The head of the legislative arm at the third tier of government in Nigeria is the
explanation:
Nigeria operates a three-tier system of government: Federal, State and Local. Each tier has its own executive and legislative branches to ensure the separation of powers.

At the local level, the legislative arm consists of elected Councillors. While the executive arm is headed by the Chairman, the legislative arm (the Council) is presided over by one of the councillors who is elected by their peers to serve as the Leader of the House (or Council Leader). The Leader coordinates the making of "bye-laws," which are the specific laws governing the local government area.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: Political power in a democracy is derived from the
explanation:
Democracy is literally defined as "rule of the people" (from the Greek demos meaning people and kratos meaning power).  In a democratic system, the citizens of the country are the only source of power.

People exercise this power by electing representatives to govern on their behalf. This is often summarized by the phrase "government of the people, by the people, and for the people".

subject: Government
q: The Secretariat of the Commonwealth of Nations is located in
explanation: The international headquarters of the Commonwealth Secretariat is located in Marlborough House. Marlborough House is situated on Pall Mall in London, United Kingdom. It has served as the Commonwealth's headquarters since 1959 and hosts various independence negotiations and high-level conferences.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations II- League of Nations, UNO/ UN, OPEC, The Commonwealth of Nations

subject: Government
q: By composition, ECOWAS can be regarded as a
explanation: ECOWAS (Economic Community of West African States) is a group of fifteen countries located specifically in the West African sub-region. It was founded in 1975 to promote economic integration and cooperation among these neighboring countries. It is considered one of the regional pillars of the broader African Economic Community.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations I - OAU, AU, ECOWAS, APPO

subject: Government
q: The recruitment, promotion and discipline of civil servants is a key function of the
explanation:
Under the Nigerian Constitution, the Federal Civil Service Commission (FCSC) has the sole authority to appoint, promote and discipline federal civil servants.

It sets uniform guidelines for staff management and serves as an appellate body for petitions regarding employment matters within the civil service. Its functions are designed to ensure that recruitment and progression are based on merit while adhering to the Federal Character principle.

subject: Government
q: The umbrella body for relations among all African countries is
explanation: The African Union (AU) is the primary continental organisation of African states. It consists of the 55 member states that make up the countries of the African continent. It was officially launched in 2002 as the successor to the Organisation of African Unity (OAU).

subject: Government
q: Party coalition is common in a
explanation: A party coalition occurs when multiple political parties join together to form a governing alliance. This is common in multi-party systems where no single party wins an absolute majority of seats in the legislature.

subject: Government
q: The Babangida transition programme to civil rule officially started in
explanation: The Babangida transition programme to civil rule officially started in 1987. General Ibrahim Babangida launched the political transition program in July 1987, which included the creation of two political parties (NRC and SDP).

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: A system of government that allows executive powers to be vested in one person
explanation: A system of government that allows executive powers to be vested in one person is the Presidential System. In this system, a single head of government (the President) leads an executive branch that is separate from the legislature.

subject: Government
q: Public corporations are funded by the
explanation: Public corporations (also known as state-owned enterprises or statutory bodies) are business organisations established by an Act of Parliament or the government to provide essential services to the public. Because they are owned by the state, their primary source of initial capital and ongoing financial support comes from government grants, budgetary allocations or loans.

subject: Government
q: The Casablanca group in the formation of the OAU was known as
explanation:
The Casablanca Group, formed in January 1961, was a bloc of African states, including Ghana, Guinea, Mali, Egypt, and Morocco, that was widely known for its radical approach to Pan-Africanism.

Led by prominent figures like Kwame Nkrumah of Ghana, the group advocated for:

  • Immediate Political Unification: They believed Africa should form a single, politically united federation immediately, referred to as the "United States of Africa".

  • A Supranational Authority: They proposed transferring significant powers from individual national governments to a central African authority.

  • A Unified Military: Nkrumah famously argued for a pan-African army to fight colonialism and protect the continent's sovereignty.

  • Deep Integration: They wanted a common currency, a central bank and a unified market right away.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community
International Organisations I - OAU, AU, ECOWAS, APPO

subject: Government
q: Public opinion  is difficult to measure because it can be
explanation:
Measuring public opinion is an inexact science highly susceptible to manipulation through various means:

  • Leading Questions: Pollsters can frame or word questions in a biased way to "manufacture" a desired response.

  • Media and Elite Influence: Politicians and interest groups often use rhetoric, advertising or misinformation to sway public perception before it is even measured.

  • Social Desirability: Respondents may lie or provide "insincere" answers to appear informed or to align with what they think is socially acceptable, making it hard to capture true beliefs.

subject: Government
q: When a pressure group is made up of people of the same profession, it is called
explanation: An occupation (or professional) group consists of individuals who share the same vocation and organise to protect their professional interests. Common examples include the Nigerian Bar Association (NBA) for lawyers or the Nigerian Union of Teachers (NUT).

subject: Government
q: The application of the rule of law can be hindered by
explanation: The rule of law requires that every person is equal before the law. Diplomatic immunity creates a hindrance to this principle because it legally exempts foreign diplomats from the criminal and civil jurisdiction of their host country.

subject: Government
q: The Nigerian military cited which of the reasons for taking over the government in the country?
options: poor funding of military operations and a lack of allocation
mismanagement of public funds by the elected political leaders
intervention of foreign nations in Nigeria's politics and public fund
lack of credible political leaders and political party system
explanation: The Nigerian military cited mismanagement of public funds by the elected political leaders as a reason for taking over the government. Military coup makers accused civilian administrations of being "infested with corruption" and gross mismanagement of national resources. They claimed their intervention was necessary to "return sanity" to public governance and rid the country of "political profiteers" and "swindlers".

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: Adjudication is the primary responsibility of the
explanation: Adjudication is the formal process by which a judge or court resolves legal disputes and interprets laws to reach a verdict. Under the doctrine of separation of powers, the Judiciary adjudicates (interprets laws), the Legislature makes laws and the Executive enforces them.

topics.title: The Workings of Government I - Organs & Systems

subject: Government
q: Which of the following promotes private ownership of properties?
explanation: Capitalism is an economic system fundamentally based on private ownership of the means of production and the pursuit of individual profit. In socialism or communalism, ownership is typically collective or state-managed rather than private.

subject: Government
q: The fundamental objective guiding a nation's foreign policy is
explanation: Foreign policy consists of the strategies a state uses to interact with other nations. All specific foreign policy goals, such as defence, security and economic benefit, are ultimately shaped by the overarching national interest of the state.

topics.title: Foreign Policy and Nigeria's Relationship with the International Community

subject: Government
q: The maintenance of peace, order and cleanliness in Igbo society was a function of
explanation: Traditionally, the age-grade system (uke) in Igboland was responsible for crime prevention, law enforcement, sanitation and community development. They served as a formal mechanism for resolving conflicts and ensuring the stability and cleanliness of the community.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: A law made by local government and public corporation and empowered by an Act of Parliament is
explanation: A bye-law is a set of rules established by a non-sovereign body (like a municipality or corporation) to regulate itself as allowed by a higher authority, such as an Act of Parliament.

subject: Government
q: The party system that was operational in Nigeria under the aborted Third Republic was the
explanation: Under the transition program of General Ibrahim Babangida for the Third Republic, the military government decreed a two-party system consisting of only the Social Democratic Party (SDP) and the National Republican Convention (NRC).

subject: Government
q: Decentralisation of power is a feature of a
explanation: In a federal state, power is constitutionally shared and decentralised between a central government and sub-national units (like states or provinces), ensuring that no single level of government has absolute control.

subject: Government
q: Written constitutions are always
explanation:
Written constitutions are generally considered rigid because they require a special, difficult, legal process to be amended, unlike ordinary laws.

While it is common for written constitutions to be rigid, they can occasionally be flexible. For example, New Zealand has a written constitution (composed of various statutes like the Constitution Act 1986) that is considered flexible because it can be amended by a simple majority vote in Parliament, much like ordinary legislation.

subject: Government
q: A permanent civil service
explanation: A permanent civil service is composed of non-political, career officials who remain in office even when elected governments change. Their permanence ensures that administrative expertise and the day-to-day functions of the state continue smoothly during transitions between different political administrations.

subject: Government
q: A politically independent nation is referred to as a
explanation:
A state is defined as a politically organised community that possesses sovereignty (political independence) over a specific territory and population.

Key Characteristics of a State

For a "politically independent nation" to be classified as a state, it generally must possess four essential elements:

  • Population: A permanent group of people residing within its borders.

  • Territory: A clearly defined geographical area with recognised boundaries.

  • Government: An organised political machinery through which the state’s will is formulated and expressed.

  • Sovereignty: The supreme authority to make and enforce laws without interference from any external power.

subject: Government
q: Before the 1976 reforms, local government administration in Nigeria was characterised by
explanation: Before 1976, local governments in Nigeria were not uniform and lacked power. They were heavily controlled by the state (regional) governments and traditional authorities. This high centralisation at the state level meant that local councils had very little independence or functional capacity.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: One of the gains of the nationalist struggles in Nigeria was
explanation:
One of the most significant gains and tools of the nationalist movement in Nigeria was the formation of political parties, which provided a structured platform to agitate for self-rule.

The introduction of the elective principle in the 1922 Clifford Constitution led to the formation of Nigeria’s first political party, the Nigerian National Democratic Party (NNDP), founded by Herbert Macaulay in 1923.

These parties (such as the NCNC, Action Group, and NPC) were essential for mobilising the masses, increasing political consciousness and negotiating constitutional reforms that eventually led to independence on October 1, 1960.

The struggles also resulted in increased Nigerian participation in governance, higher education (like Yaba Higher College), and constitutional changes that shifted power from the British to Nigerians.

subject: Government
q: The minister of defence in Hausa pre-colonial political system is
explanation:
In the highly centralised Hausa/Fulani emirate system, the Emir was assisted by several ministers with specific roles:

  • Madawaki (or Madaki): He was the commander of the army and acted as the Minister of Defense.

  • Galadima: He was the administrator of the capital city.

  • Sarkin Pawa: He was the head of all butchers.

  • Dogari: He served as the head of the police or royal bodyguards.

topics.title: Political and Constitutional Development in Nigeria

subject: Government
q: A type of government defined by equal representation, credible elections and the sanctity of the ballot box is
explanation:
Democracy is a system of government based on the principle that power belongs to the people. It is characterised by:

  • Equal Representation: Every citizen has an equal opportunity to influence decisions through representatives.

  • Credible Elections: Periodic, free and fair elections allow the public to choose their leaders.

  • Sanctity of the Ballot Box: This refers to the protection of the voting process (such as the use of a secret ballot) to ensure the results accurately reflect the will of the people.

subject: Government
q: A remarkable feature of the Republican Constitution was the
options: The president was the head of government
The prime minister also performs ceremonial functions
Privy Court was the final court of arbiter
The queen ceased to be the Head of State
explanation:
The 1963 Republican Constitution ended Nigeria's relationship with the British monarchy; the Queen of England was no longer the head of state and was replaced by an elected President.

While the president became the head of state, the Prime Minister remained the head of government, as the constitution retained the parliamentary system. Another key feature was making the Supreme Court of Nigeria the final court of appeal, replacing the British Privy Council.

subject: Government
q: The declaration of Africa as the centrepiece of Nigeria's foreign policy could be traced back to the administration of
explanation:
While General Yakubu Gowon's regime is often credited with formalising the "centrepiece" terminology in the 1970s, the policy itself was rooted in the administration of Sir Abubakar Tafawa Balewa.

Upon independence in 1960, Balewa declared that Africa would be the primary concern of Nigeria's foreign policy. His commitment was evidenced by his leading role in forming the Organisation of African Unity (OAU) in 1963.

subject: Government
q: Public corporations unlike private enterprises
explanation: Unlike private enterprises, which are established to make profit, public corporations (like the old NEPA or Water Boards) are created by the government to provide social and essential services at affordable rates to the citizens.

subject: Government
q: OPEC was formed by some oil producing nations in
explanation: The Organisation of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) was founded in September 1960 at the Baghdad Conference by five founding members: Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, and Venezuela.

subject: Government
q: The constitution that allowed forty-six legislative members was the
explanation:
A new Legislative Council was established exclusively for Southern Nigeria under the Clifford Constitution. It consisted of:

  • 46 members in total.
    • 27 official members (colonial administrators).
    • 19 unofficial members, including:
      • 9 Europeans (mainly representatives of commercial interests).
      • 10 Africans
        • 4 were elected (3 elected from Lagos, 1 from Calabar).
        • 6 were appointed by the governor to represent other parts of the country

subject: Government
q: OMBUDSMAN is usually used to represent
explanation: In Nigeria, the Ombudsman is represented by the Public Complaints Commission (PCC). Its role is to investigate and resolve complaints by citizens against administrative injustice or the abuse of power by government officials.
